comments.text: For the second result from [Er55d] $P(n, k) \gg k \log k$, what is the role of $c$ there? Also, I couldn't locate this problem (or [961]) in [Er79]; probably I'm missing something.
I think you misread the reference, it's [Er79d] not [Er79] (it is on p.74 of [Er79d]). The $c$ is just a typo on my part, I'll remove it.
Minor point, but we may restrict to $1 \le k \le n-1$, since $P(1)$ is not well-defined? (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

It seems easy to construct a counterexample to the main problem. Take $n=2^t$ and $k=n-1$ $(t\geq2)$. Then $\binom{n}{k}=\binom{2^t}{1}=2^t$, so $P\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)=2$. Moreover $\min(n-k+1,\;k^{1+c})=\min(2,(2^t-1)^{1+c})=2$. Hence $P\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)=2\not>2$, so $(n,k)=(2^t,2^t-1)$ is a counterexample for every $c>0$. It seems as an infinite series of counterexamples to the main problem? Or I've missed/misunderstood something. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Given the Sylvester-Schur theorem and the symmetry $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}$, it seems likely here that Erdos intended to write $\geq \min(n-k+1, \dots)$ here instead of $> \min(n-k,\dots)$ (or alternatively, to drop the "+1").

As Tao points out, this is just a typo - really the annoying $n-k+1$ term is just to handle $n/2<k\leq n$ in a non-trivial way. One should really view these problems as restricted to $k\leq n/2$. As mentioned, this is just the same as [961] (which is stated without binomial coefficients). Including both was an oversight on my part, but it feels like it would mess up too much to remove this one now.
